other_side: Zitron's answer to the GLM 5.2 excitement is that getting thrilled about another model topping a leaderboard is precisely the trap. His piece argues the opposite of a flowering: AI is, in his words, "slowing down just as it needs to speed up," and no amount of "rigged benchmarks and anecdotal examples" changes the fact that you still cannot measure the cost of a task or its return - open-weight or closed. The real story, on his telling, is enterprises capping token spend because they cannot link bills to value: Uber blew its annual budget in a quarter, Brex limits engineers to a few hundred dollars a week. And underneath sits an infrastructure build demanding trillions in annual revenue by 2030 he sees no path to, with barely any paying demand outside two loss-making labs. Whether GLM is good is, to him, beside the point.
truth_beneath: Both men orbit one variable: whether a fresh model release like GLM 5.2 is a signal that matters, or a distraction. Both accept the same facts - the benchmarks and practitioner raves are real, and enterprises are capping AI spend because cost-per-task and ROI are hard to measure. The empirical question that will resolve it is whether cheaper, diverse, open models convert into measurable, sustained value and a widening base of paying demand; the observable is enterprise AI spend, renewals, and whether real revenue emerges outside OpenAI and Anthropic. The residue is pure worldview: Whittemore reads proliferation as healthy maturation, Zitron as rearranging deckchairs on an unfinanceable build-out. Whittemore is vindicated if diverse models drive durable productivity and paying demand broadens; Zitron if spend stays capped and demand stays concentrated in two loss-makers, whatever the model. Both are polemicists with incentives - Whittemore bullish and anti-doom, Zitron bubble-certain and anti-hype with a newsletter - so the signal is their shared premise that ROI is currently unmeasurable. Watch whether profitable AI demand appears outside the two big labs.
